findings.message: Unbounded table scan: `select(Item.id)` has no LIMIT, so every row in `items` is fetched into application memory. The sibling endpoint enforces MAX_PAGE_SIZE=100 via `list_items`; this endpoint has no equivalent guard. On a large table this materialises the full ID set in RAM before a single name is returned.
findings.suggestion: Add `.limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)` (imported from `repository`) to the ID query, or accept `limit`/`offset` query params matching the convention on `GET /items`. Better still, collapse to one query (see N+1 finding below) and apply the limit there.

findings.message: N+1 query: `session.get(Item, iid)` inside a loop issues one SELECT per row. After fetching N IDs on line 44, this fires N additional round-trips — O(N) database queries for a single HTTP request. The docstring acknowledges it, but the code ships it.
findings.suggestion: Replace both lines 44–49 with a single query: `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id).limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)))`. One query, zero loop, bounded result.

findings.message: Connection-pool exhaustion under load: the SQLAlchemy session (and its underlying DBAPI connection) is held open for the entire N-iteration loop, each iteration issuing a synchronous round-trip. Under concurrent traffic, P parallel requests each holding a connection for N round-trips can exhaust the connection pool, causing new requests to queue or fail. This compounds with the unbounded read on line 44.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single query (eliminating the loop) so each request holds the connection for exactly one round-trip. If pagination cannot be added immediately, at minimum cap N with `.limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)` to bound the hold time.
